speaker: interviewer
text: Is this chair-- do you think it'll be close enough for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: What about this one? This is when we were already married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, that's lovely. That's fine.

speaker: interviewee
text: I just found it here. It saved me dragging down--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, don't drag anything down, no. OK, that's-- we'll put it with the other photos.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you've got the others there. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where are they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, who else hasn't got a glass?

speaker: interviewer
text: This survivor-- sorry, today's date is May--

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Today's date is May the 23rd, 1995. Survivor's name is Maurice Kochan. Interviewer's name is Litzi Hart. It's in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the language is in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Litzi Hart, L-I-T-Z-I H-A-R-T. And it is-- the date is the 23rd of May, 1995, and I'm conducting an interview, with Maurice Kochan, in New South Wales-- Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. OK, Maurice, could you-- could you say and spell your name, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Maurice Kochan, Maurice, M-A-U-R-I-C-E. My Christian name is K-O-C-H-A-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's your surname?

speaker: interviewee
text: My surname, sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. That's all right. And how was it pronounced originally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ko-han.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ko-han. And could I have your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the 10th of April, 1927.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old does that make you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Beg you's?

speaker: interviewer
text: How old does that make you?

speaker: interviewer
text: 68. And where were you born, the city and the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Wadowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: In?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland. You were 12 when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you tell me your first memories of your childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, I-- I remember when the war started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before? Yes, all I can remember is that I attended school, and I attend a Jewish school, as well. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I used to enjoy sport. And I used to go to a club, which calls Maccabi. And I had a very good childhood. I got-- had lovely parents. And I enjoyed my childhood.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had three brothers and four sisters.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. And could you tell me a bit more about your school? Could you remember your first day at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't. The first, I don't remember. But I do remember being at school and playing soccer and playing all kind of things. That I do remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what sort of a school was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a public school. It was in the city, in Wadowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a Jewish school, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there many Jewish children in that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were quite a few Jewish children, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you remember being at all-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] any signs of antisemitism, at all, in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- not at school. But I do remember antisemitism in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can you describe, say, a typical day at home and at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, as I said, we were seven children. And we were, I must say, all very, very good children. And we had lovely parents, who enjoyed, especially, Friday nights. Because my parents were not fanatics, but they were religion people. They-- my father used to go to Saturday, and I used to come with him to shul every Saturday and on Friday night. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go with your brothers, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm sure, my brothers went too. But I always remember going with my father, walking, yes. But my brothers went. They all went to shul. They-- on days that we went to shul. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went to school-- did you speak Polish at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Polish, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other languages?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not Yiddish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, very seldom. And we couldn't speak Yiddish. I learned Yiddish, to speak, after the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. And whereabouts did you live? What sort of a district was it that you lived in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived not far from the-- from the German border, that was southwest of Germany, not far from the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the town again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wadowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near the German border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not far from the German border, because we had another city, Bielsko, which was not far from us. And there, it was the border.

speaker: interviewer
text: The border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And which was the biggest city, the closest to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cracow?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you tell me more about your school, what you remember there? Did you have friends there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I had lots of friends at school. Not only Jewish people, I had lots of-- lots of friends, Polish people. And I enjoyed my school, very, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you bring them home, your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. We used to-- they used to come to my place. I used to go to their place, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: To the non-Jewish ones, as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and did your parents also mix with non-Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did. We had quite Polish friends that they used to come to our place, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they led a fairly open sort of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because Wadowice didn't have as many Jews. There were not many Jews in our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe a couple of thousand Jews. But not many. I'm not even sure if there were 2,000 families by then. We were not a big Jewish community. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And then could you tell me a bit about your Jewish-- the Jewish school you went to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I remember going to cheder. I remember, like today, I was pulling a-- still a little toy when I started going to cheder. And I-- I was-- was going there for a little while. And then I was quite a naughty boy, so my father brought the teacher home and he used to teach me at home.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you mean, you were a naughty boy?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] Well, you know, how it used to be in my time. We used to tear-- [LAUGHS] tear all the buttons from our things. We used to play with it. And-- and I didn't behave the way my parents would like me behave, so they brought the teacher home, and I used to-- till the end, till the war came, I had this teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: At home? Did you like to-- learning?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much, yes. Very much, yes. I even remember his name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, what was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was Alster.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alster

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wow. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And what do you remember next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's from the Jewish thing. The--

speaker: interviewer
text: At what stage do you remember any changes in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in-- in-- it might be a week or so before the war, when they started talking about it. I-- I remember that the panic that it was coming on the city. And then, I-- I remember when it started.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you understand what was going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I knew there was going to be a war, but I didn't understand too much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your parents talk about it at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they did talk about it, because they were thinking what they're going to do if the war would come. And matter of fact, when the war started, it was on a Friday night. And on a Saturday morning, my father was trying to arrange a-- though he was religious, but he was trying to arrange a car, because he wanted to leave, not to be not so close to the border. And he wanted to get away, further away from it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he rented a car. And we were all there, waiting, and my mother disappeared. And we didn't know where mum was.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what she did-- see my father understood. He was a religious person. So was my mother, but my father, religiously, he knew more than-- than my mother did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he-- she-- what she did, she went to the rabbi, in the city, to ask him if he would allow her to go, on a Saturday, by car? So he said, if I would have a car, I would go, too. And so she came, and she went with us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And tell me, just before we go on with that-- and I want to get on with that-- can you tell me a bit more about your brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. One-- one brother-- What, from '39?

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, before.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were the youngest of the family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was the youngest in the family. I really don't remember too much about them. I really don't. So I don't like to say something that I really don't remember it. All I can say, I-- I was the baby.

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- and that's why I'm interested in your impressions or any impressions. Not so much definite memories, but things that you might-- things that-- images, you know? Just impressions of-- of your family, what you felt, the sort of things that, as a young boy, you felt secure.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was very secure. I was very secure, because we were quite well off, you know. And I was secure in every way. I wasn't missing on anything when I was a child. I always could get a few-- there were zlotys in Poland. Well, I-- I always had a few zlotys with me, to go to school, to buy things. So I never missed anything as a child. And I was the youngest. I was spoiled.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you-- did you play much with your brothers and sisters? Or was the age gap too--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. I played with the other children, outside, not with my brothers and sisters. The youngest sister, yes, but not with the others anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right And how did your parents make their living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a menswear-- a menswear store and a liquor restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Were they two separate businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were two separate business, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were your brothers involved in it at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were involved in it, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: IN what-- how many of them were--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were two brothers, yes, and there was an elder sister, as well, the eldest sister. But the brothers were involved in it, more than anything, with the parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: So can we then, now, come back to what happened on the-- when you first noticed changes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, on the-- on the first night, when the war broke out, as I said before, we tried to get a car to get away from the city. And we were very, very lucky, because, on the Saturday morning, the restaurant that we had, if it wouldn't be a Saturday, some of the family would be dead today. But they would die then, because the first bomb that fell in our city was on our restaurant.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were lucky, because Saturday it was closed. We didn't open the shop on a Saturday. So that was closed. And the people that they lived there were killed.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then we went by car. We went to Dzialoszyce--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: --to a a different city.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 100 and-- about 120-- roughly 120 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dzialoszyce. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And when we arrived to Dzialoszyce, we rent a little house. It only had a kitchen and a room. And we were seven children, where we came from three bedrooms, which in Poland was quite a-- a comfortable house. And we were all living in one-- one bedroom and a kitchen place. So we were-- some of them, at night, we were on the floors, just having a bed made-up. And we all slept together.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I wasn't frightened, because, at the time, we haven't seen the Germans yet, there. It took a while, a few days, by the time the Germans arrived. And I even remember when the Germans arrived. I went to look, seen the tank coming in. You know, they were talking. We didn't realize what-- what's going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nobody realized?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nobody knew anything there, you know? I remember, even like today, there-- one of the ladies was saying that they're-- they're lovely people. They're nice people. No, we really didn't realize what-- what ahead of us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, if you think back, what sort of signs did you have before then, did you-- with antisemitism, for example? What did you notice about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in Poland, there was always antisemitism. It was always antisemitism. But me being so young, I maybe didn't take so much notice. But there was, because I remember always calling names on Jews, you know, and things. But in Poland, they were very much antisemitic, because I must say that I was afraid more of the Poles then I was afraid of the Germans, because the Germans wouldn't recognize you as a Jew. They couldn't say that you are definitely a Jew. And the Poles, he looked at you, and he could point out, right away, that you were Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get into fights at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really, no. No, not really. No, but the-- and it was quite big antisemitism in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, you go at school, and the next day you weren't? Is that how it worked?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in '39, as soon as the Germans came in, as they walked into Dzialoszyce, the first thing they did, after a few days, close all the schools for Jewish children. There were no schools for Jewish children. And this is the only thing that I'm missing, because all I could finish was five classes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, but it wasn't a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: There wasn't a Jewish school, no. But Jews were not allowed to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not allowed to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was just, you know? Maybe as a child, I was happy that I didn't go to school. I don't know. I don't remember those things. But maybe so. But things started to get, from one day-- worse from one day to the next. And it was really just terrible. You know, more Germans came into the city. And we didn't know what to do, because we had the home in Wadowice. And we had now a home in Dzialoszyce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the Germans already arrived, my parents decided that my mother and three sisters would go back to Wadowice and look after the home in Wadowice.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we would stay here. And we see, maybe, it won't take too long, so we all go back together. But unfortunately, it didn't happen. So the-- it wasn't for much-- yeah, but what-- what happened is the-- the worry my father had, because we couldn't receive any mail from them. And he started to worry terribly that what happened to them. Because there was no mail going there. And all we knew that they arrived all right home. But a week went by, two weeks, three weeks, and we didn't have any mail from them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were really-- he was really worrying. And-- and I could see my father, what he was going through. And I suggest, once, to my father, that, if he would buy me a bicycle, I would go. Because I'm the youngest, and the Germans wouldn't do anything to a-- a young boy like this. So he didn't want to hear about it. And my other brother, which was with us too, he-- he wouldn't go. He was-- he was-- he was afraid. He realized, more than me, that it's not so easy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I loved bicycles. I used to love-- I had a bicycle, always. And I loved the bicycle. And my father wouldn't be interested in it. But then another couple weeks went by, and he didn't hear anything. And I begged him, because I could see really what he was-- how he was suffering. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: So, [SOBBING] and he decided that he would buy me a bicycle. And he bought me a bicycle. And I got up at 4:00 in the morning, because I told my father that I want to get up very early, because I was terribly scared at night.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was terribly scared at night. And I didn't know how long it will take me, because I had to 220 kilometers. So I got up very early in the morning, and I went. My father was crying, and I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] You were 12?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SOBBING] I was 12 years of age, yes. [CRYING] On the way, I was-- I was doing quite all right. And Germans were, by the thousands, passing me. And no one stopped me at all. I was just doing all right. I was about 15 kilometers from home or 20 kilometers from home, I can't remember exactly. And I was the happiest person, because I still had plenty of time. Because I was afraid it will get dark. So I had plenty of time. But then, as I came closer, someone was shouting, this boy on the bicycle is a Jew-- a Pole. So I was really scared that the Germans will hear something, and I could be really in trouble. But I was going so fast. And I arrived home. When I arrived home, I didn't even enter the home, and we had a dog, and he was barking like he would know that I'm coming. And when they opened the door for me, he jumped on me. I remember like today. You know, he hasn't seen us for quite a long time. And my mother was there and the three sisters. So I had a shower. And my mother gave me something to eat. I think I told her that I can't stay, because she wanted me to stay a few days. I said, no, because father's worrying terribly, and I have to go first thing in the morning. I have to go straight back to give him the  news.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my mother said, how can you do it in-- in two days? You know, you shouldn't do it. No, but I didn't. I said, no, I promised my father that I would be back as soon as possible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the morning, I got up very early again, and my mother made me breakfast. And I went. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] On the way, right through, I had a terrific ride. I had a much better ride than I had the first time, because I knew already, and no one stopped me or anything. It was really beautiful. And I arrived-- and when I was close to home, I was the happiest person. Because I did it for my father, what he wanted me to do. And when I came home, [SOBBING] I have never seen my father-- he just took me in his hands, and he was crying. [CRYING] [SOBBING] And I was really happy that I could do this for him.

speaker: interviewer
text: [CRYING] So it took you a day, each way?

speaker: interviewee
text: That? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What hours?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did 240-- 240 kilometers. Well, I left very early in the morning. I left about, say, between 4 and 5 o'clock in the morning, because that's the one thing I wanted to do, because I was afraid, very much, at night. I wouldn't go from here to there at night. So-- and this is what I did. And I had no problem at all. 120 kilometers I made a day on the bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to your shops, the businesses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was bombed. The-- the restaurant was bombed. And the menswear, when we took a car-- we had a big car-- and we took as much as we could. We took out, to Dzialoszyce, everything from the menswear store, whatever we could. But we couldn't take everything. So we left the things. But the-- when the Germans came in, they took everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you just tell me how was the restaurant bombed? You did say. Who bombed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they came into Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they-- yes. The first-- the first bomb fell in Wadowice on this-- on our restaurant. And three people were killed there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened then, when you came back to your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I came back to my father, after-- after a while, then it took about another couple weeks or three weeks, and then mail started to-- to arrive. And we were very happy, because then even we could send them parcels. And we were quite happy. We were only hoping that the war won't last too long, and we'd be able to go back to our home. Unfortunately, it didn't happen so. And then they were trying-- you see, Wadowice belonged once to Germany in the First World War. And they were trying to-- the Germans wanted to make a border there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we heard this, we wanted mum and-- and her-- the sisters to leave there, to come to Dzialoszyce. But we didn't realize that the border, they will make it so fast.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unfortunately, the did it. And-- and they was-- they were staying there, for a while, because we had to make arrangements, how to bring them to Dzialoszyce.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was somebody in-- in Wadowice that would take us through the border, for a lot of money, which we did. And after a while, they managed to cross the border, all of them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were very lucky, because there were no Jews, anymore, in the city anywhere. The Jews were-- the Jews were in ghettos or they were in the concentration camps or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anybody who went to a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from our family.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from our-- but why I'm saying about the ghetto, because, when-- when my sister and-- and my mother went to Cracow, well, to start with, they had to wear a-- a yellow-- a star of David.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they didn't, because they-- they couldn't travel on a train. And that's what they did. So they were just risking their lives. And in Cracow, a policeman stopped them, a Polish policeman. He said, what are you looking at? Because that was the ghetto there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, nothing. We're just looking at things. And he just let them go. And then they came to Dzialoszyce. And we were the happiest people in the world, because the whole family was together again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were very happy. We were not comfortable, but-- but everybody was happy. It doesn't matter where we slept or so, but we were all together.

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one person who wasn't with us was the oldest brother, because he was in the army. But it didn't take long, and he came home, as well. They let him go. He was a prisoner of war. And he came back, so we were the whole family, and we were happy. But unfortunately, we were happy, but, every day, it was getting worse. And it was impossible to live, the way the Germans were going on with things.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, every day, there are new orders. They-- I don't know if you heard, there was a Judenrat, which-- a Jewish committee. And the Germans used to give them orders, what to do. Today, for example, they want a kilo of gold. And if not, they will kill so and so many Jews. And-- and they used to work, they-- they ask every day. And things were getting impossible to live. And then they stopped you from going outside the city. You were not allowed to go outside the city at all. Jews were not allowed to go. But we took a risk. My-- I must say, that in-- my brothers, none of them looked Jewish or the sisters. And we-- they didn't worry. They risked. And because, for menswear-- for menswear, what we had in Dzialoszyce, for one suit, you could get a lot of food. And they used to go outside the city and bring chickens or bread or anything, milk, everything for that. So we had-- really, we didn't suffer with the hunger at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they traded the clothes--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we traded, yes. And we knew--

speaker: interviewer
text: --with the Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: --lots of Poles, yes. That they were happy to do it, because you couldn't find during-- after the-- the war a suit or something like that. We had beautiful things, because, from the shop, we took everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we really didn't know-- didn't know what hunger means, because we had plenty to eat. And I'm sure lots of people suffered a terrible lot, because there was no food around in the city.

speaker: interviewee
text: And unfortunately, as the time was getting on, it was getting so bad that it was just impossible to live anymore. And they were killing people and taking people to concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we-- we heard that people were taken to Belzec or things. We found out that they were gassed. And also, people were worrying, really, that, you know, what will happen to us? When did you find that out? What year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in '42, already. In '42, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the year that you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: The year after that, we left. You see, when-- a few days before, we heard that the Germans are coming in. And they're going to liquidate the whole city, and all the Jews will go. And no one knew what will happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we heard this, we were trying to do something about it. And that was signed before we were selling to the Poles the suits, and we had quite acquaintance with them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one of the Poles, when we told him what's going to happen, he said, look, don't worry. When something will happen, you just come, and you stay at our place. So we will look after you. We've-- I've got a hiding, and you will be at our place there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were very happy. And we were discussing, with our parents, this. But my parents didn't want to go. So we were really worrying, because we could see that every day it was getting worse. And we knew that they could come any day now, the Germans. So what we did-- We-- we might stop here.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just because we're going to change--

speaker: interviewer
text: End of tape one.
